literal: exclusion, exclusion-by-removal; the setting aside of what does not belong
invariant_definition: *Apoha* is the Buddhist account of how concepts and words function without committing one to real universals. A word such as "cow" does not latch onto a positive *jāti* inhering in many particulars; it works by excluding what is other than cow. The doctrine therefore sits inside the Dignāga-Dharmakīrti *pramāṇa-dvaya* apparatus. Perception grasps only the causally efficacious *svalakṣaṇa*; conceptual cognition and language work at the level of *sāmānya-lakṣaṇa*; *apoha* explains how that conceptual level can still be usable and even epistemically valid without positing a real universal. The doctrine is not merely a semantic trick. It is a strategy for preserving Buddhist nominal anti-essentialism in logic, language, and inference. Its critics immediately press the central problem: exclusion seems to presuppose what it claims to replace, since excluding non-cows appears already to require some grasp of cowness. The history of *apoha* is largely the history of Buddhist attempts to answer that charge and realist attempts to show that the answer fails.

per_school.definition: Vācaspati Miśra states the standard Nyāya objection that positive terms such as dravya and sat cannot coherently be reduced to anyāpoha, because that would destroy their vidhirūpa force and make sāmānādhikaraṇya unintelligible. [cite://vacaspati/nyaya-varttika-tatparya-tika/2.2.65-anyapoha-vs-jati] He also reports the Buddhist thesis that words and concepts proceed as anyāpohagopacara, only to reject it in favor of real universals and real jātimatī vyakti as the object of śabda and vikalpa. [cite://vacaspati/nyaya-varttika-tatparya-tika/2.2.65-anyapoha-vs-jati]

Sucaritamiśra, in the Kāśikā on the Ślokavārttika, explicitly lists sādṛśyarūpa and apoharūpa as rival accounts of sāmānya to be rejected from the Mīmāṃsā standpoint. [cite://sucaritamisra/mimamsa-slokavarttika-kasika/2.135-137-apoharupa-samanya] He further argues that exclusion cannot operate upon what is absolutely nonexistent, so an apoha-based account cannot ground the cognition of word-meaning that the school requires. [cite://sucaritamisra/mimamsa-slokavarttika-kasika/2.135-137-apoharupa-samanya]

per_school.not_applicable_reason: In the supplied Mādhyamaka primaries for this phase, apoha does not appear as a distinct school-defining technical category, and no substantive locus was retrievable.

per_school.definition: Within the Dharmakīrtian wing of Yogācāra, the inferential sign is explicitly said to have an apohaviṣaya, since grasping one property in a real thing does not by itself amount to grasping the whole object. [cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.46-47-apohavisaya-lingam] Apoha therefore marks the conceptual object of inference and verbal determination, rather than a positively real universal inhering in particulars. [cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.46-47-apohavisaya-lingam]

Pratyabhijñā does not deploy apoha as a Buddhist denial of universals; rather, it internalizes apohana as a power of unitary consciousness and makes exclusion constitutive of vikalpa. [cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.3.7-jnana-smrti-apohana] [cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.6.3-5-anyavyapohana] Utpaladeva accordingly states that the determination 'ghaṭa' arises through atad-vyapohana, and that the body-bound aham-vimarśa is itself a vikalpa generated by anyavyapohana. [cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.6.3-5-anyavyapohana]

translator_note: "Exclusion" is standard, but it can sound too thin if read in ordinary English. The doctrine is not just saying that words are vague or contrastive. It is a full alternative to realist universal theory. Calling it simply "nominalism" is also lazy: that imports a Western debate while hiding the specifically Buddhist links to *svalakṣaṇa*, *sāmānya-lakṣaṇa*, *artha-kriyā*, and karmically conditioned conceptuality.
